heading: Change Storage Capacity and IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on Azure (Microsoft Azure)
body:
For **Low CPU** or **General** clusters deployed on Azure (Microsoft Azure),
you can extend IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) and the data volumes' storage capacity in the Atlas UI.

If you increase disk storage for **Low CPU** or **General**
clusters, you can:

- Receive more IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on clusters in the same cluster tier.

- Use these clusters for hosting applications with high IO workload requirements
  without having to configure sharding to spread the applications' workload across
  servers.

> **Note:** Not all Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions support extended storage. For details,
> see [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).

Azure (Microsoft Azure) has volume modification cooldown periods that might limit how
frequently you can make storage changes.

> **Important:** Azure (Microsoft Azure) restricts disk IOPS/throughput adjustments to a maximum of four changes
> within a 24-hour period for Pv2 disks. After this limit is reached, MongoDB
> replaces the virtual machine (VM) to bypass this limitation.

The following statements describe the options you have in the Atlas UI
for IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) and storage management on Azure (Microsoft Azure) clusters.

- Initially, when you choose an Azure (Microsoft Azure) **Low CPU** or
  **General** cluster, Atlas assigns the default storage
  size and IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) based on the tier you choose.

- Depending on the specific characteristics of the storage volumes used
  in your region and whether the cluster is in the
  [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
  you can do one of the following actions:

  - If your region doesn't [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
    you can change storage capacity only in preset amounts and can't change
    the IOPS using the slider.

  - If your region [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
    you can use the **Storage** slider to increase the default storage
    capacity within the same tier. Atlas also increases IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) to correspond
    to the increased storage size.

- If, after you increase the storage volume's size, the increased number
  of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) still isn't sufficient, and the cluster's tier is in a
  [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions),
  you can further extend the number of IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) using the **IOPS** slider,
  without changing the cluster tier.

- In addition, you can also expand the total cluster storage beyond
  the default limits for the cluster's tier in
  [regions that support extended storage](microsoft-azure-storage-supported-regions).
  You enable extended storage sizes in the [project settings](project-settings).

> **Note:** When increasing IOPS for a cluster deployed on Azure, values are limited to the cluster tier's maximum IOPS.

The following sections describe how to increase the storage size and IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second).

- [increase](change-storage-azure-steps)

- [Extend IOPS (Input/Output Operations per Second) on Azure (Microsoft Azure)](extend-iops-azure-steps)
